mitre_attack: T1552.001 - Unsecured Credentials: Credentials In Files
cwe: CWE-316 - Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information in Memory
CWE-312 - Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
CWE-359 - Exposure of Private Personal Information to an Unauthorized Actor
CWE-200 - Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

threat.title: Memory Scraping and RAM Dumping for Payment Card Data Theft
threat.description: Memory scraping malware specifically targets point-of-sale (POS) systems, payment terminals, and e-commerce applications to extract unencrypted payment card data (Track 1/Track 2 data, CVV, cardholder names) from system RAM during transaction processing. Unlike general memory dumps, RAM scrapers use sophisticated pattern matching to identify magnetic stripe data formats, continuously scan memory for credit card patterns, and exfiltrate data before it's encrypted for transmission. This attack vector was responsible for some of the largest retail breaches including Target (110M cards), Home Depot (56M cards), and affects payment processors, retail POS systems, hospitality industry, and e-commerce platforms running vulnerable payment processing software.

related_frameworks: PCI DSS Requirement 3 (Protect Stored Cardholder Data)

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker gains initial access to retail network through phishing, compromised vendor credentials, or exploiting unpatched POS vulnerabilities
2. Deploys memory scraper malware (e.g., BlackPOS, Backoff, Alina, JackPOS) to POS terminals via lateral movement
3. Malware installs as persistent service/scheduled task and injects into payment processing application memory space
4. Continuously scans process memory (every 100-500ms) for Track 1/Track 2 magnetic stripe patterns using regex: ^%B[0-9]{13,19}\^[^\^]+\^[0-9]{4} (Track 1) or ;[0-9]{13,19}=[0-9]{4} (Track 2)
5. Captures cardholder data from RAM before encryption: Primary Account Number (PAN), expiration date, cardholder name, service code, CVV
6. Exfiltrates stolen data via DNS tunneling, HTTP POST to C2 servers, or stores encrypted locally for batch upload
7. Attackers sell card data on dark web marketplaces (Joker's Stash, rescator.la) for $20-100 per card depending on card type and country
prerequisites: Access to retail/hospitality network with POS systems
POS system running vulnerable Windows OS (XP, 7, 10 Embedded)
Payment card data temporarily stored unencrypted in memory during transaction authorization
Lack of Point-to-Point Encryption (P2PE) from card swipe to processor
Insufficient network segmentation between POS and corporate networks
Missing endpoint detection/EDR on POS terminals
Local administrator privileges on POS terminal for malware installation

cve_references.description: Shellshock - Bash vulnerability used in POS breaches to gain initial access via vulnerable web interfaces on payment terminals
Pulse Secure VPN arbitrary file reading vulnerability exploited to access retail networks and deploy POS malware
Nagios XI privilege escalation used to compromise monitoring systems and pivot to POS networks
Laravel PHP framework deserialization vulnerability exploited to compromise e-commerce checkout pages for memory scraping

real_world_breaches.impact: 110 million customer records compromised including 40 million credit/debit cards; $292 million total costs; CEO resignation; $18.5 million settlement with 47 states
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers used stolen HVAC vendor credentials to access Target network, moved laterally to POS systems, deployed BlackPOS RAM scraper malware that captured Track 1/Track 2 data from memory during card swipes. Malware used regex pattern matching to identify magnetic stripe data formats and exfiltrated data via internal FTP server to attacker-controlled systems.

real_world_breaches.incident: Home Depot Data Breach (Backoff POS Malware)
real_world_breaches.impact: 56 million payment cards stolen over 5 months; 53 million customer email addresses exposed; $179 million in costs; $19.5 million settlement
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Attackers exploited stolen vendor credentials to access network, then deployed Backoff RAM scraper malware to self-checkout POS systems. Malware scraped memory for Track 1/Track 2 data patterns, used custom encryption for C2 communication, and maintained persistence via Windows services.

real_world_breaches.impact: 130 million credit/debit cards compromised; largest payment processor breach in history at the time; $140 million in settlements; company nearly bankrupted
real_world_breaches.root_cause: SQL injection attack gained access to corporate network, attackers installed RAM scraper sniffer malware on payment processing servers that captured card data during authorization processing before encryption. Malware used pattern matching for PAN/Track data and exfiltrated via encrypted channels to Eastern European servers.

real_world_breaches.impact: 350,000 payment cards compromised; 9,200 cards used fraudulently; luxury retail chain targeted; estimated $5 million in fraud losses
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Custom memory scraping malware deployed to POS terminals scraped RAM for payment card data. Attack ran for 8 months undetected due to lack of POS monitoring and network segmentation.

real_world_breaches.impact: 216 restaurant locations affected; estimated 2 million payment cards stolen; multi-year breach spanning 2014-2016
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Point-of-sale systems infected with Backoff malware variant that scraped memory for Track 1/Track 2 data. Inadequate POS security controls and lack of P2PE encryption allowed prolonged undetected compromise.

real_world_breaches.incident: Saks Fifth Avenue / Lord & Taylor Breach (JokerStash)
real_world_breaches.impact: 5 million payment cards stolen from 83 Saks Fifth Avenue and 216 Lord & Taylor stores; cards sold on Joker's Stash dark web marketplace
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Memory scraping malware (suspected JackPOS or Alina variant) infected Windows-based POS terminals, captured unencrypted payment data from RAM during transaction processing. Lack of end-to-end encryption (P2PE) from swipe to processor allowed data capture.

real_world_breaches.impact: 850 locations affected; approximately 30 million payment cards compromised over 9 months; cards used for fraudulent purchases totaling millions
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Memory scraping malware deployed to fuel pump and in-store POS systems. Malware captured card data from memory before encryption, maintained persistence, and exfiltrated data to attacker infrastructure. Breach discovered by law enforcement notification, not internal monitoring.

real_world_breaches.impact: Undisclosed number of payment cards from online and in-store transactions; lasted 10 days before detection
real_world_breaches.root_cause: Third-party wallet application used on POS systems was compromised. Memory scraper captured payment information during checkout before encryption.

bug_bounty_reports.summary: E-commerce checkout page JavaScript loads payment card data into browser memory unencrypted, vulnerable to memory scraping via browser extensions or malicious scripts
POS application stores full magnetic stripe Track 1/Track 2 data in plaintext in process memory after transaction completion, violating PCI DSS 3.4
Payment terminal software logs full PAN and CVV to memory-mapped log file accessible by low-privilege users

security_research.title: RAM Scrapers: How Point-of-Sale Malware Works
security_research.key_finding: Analyzed 15 POS malware families including BlackPOS, Backoff, Alina, Dexter. All use regex pattern matching for Track 1/Track 2 data: Track 1 starts with %B followed by 13-19 digits, Track 2 starts with ; followed by 13-19 digits. Malware scans memory every 100-500ms and captures data in 2-3 second window when unencrypted in RAM.

security_research.title: The Evolution of POS Malware: From BlackPOS to ModPipe
security_research.key_finding: POS malware has evolved from simple memory scanners to sophisticated multi-stage frameworks. Modern variants use process hollowing, reflective DLL injection, and encrypt exfiltration via DNS tunneling. 89% of POS malware targets Windows XP/7 embedded systems still in production.

security_research.title: Memory Scraping Techniques in Financial Malware
security_research.key_finding: Memory scraping is T1005 (Data from Local System) technique. Used in 78% of retail breaches 2013-2020. Mitigation requires P2PE (Point-to-Point Encryption) from card reader hardware through entire authorization chain, eliminating cleartext windows.

security_research.title: PCI DSS 4.0: Enhanced Requirements for Memory Scraping Protection
security_research.key_finding: PCI DSS 4.0 Requirement 3.5.1 now mandates that PAN must never be stored unencrypted in volatile memory except during active authorization processing (maximum 5 seconds). Requirement 11.6.1 requires deployment of change detection mechanisms on POS systems to detect memory scraper installation.

security_research.title: BlackPOS/Kaptoxa Malware Analysis
security_research.key_finding: BlackPOS (aka Kaptoxa) uses CreateToolhelp32Snapshot to enumerate processes, reads memory via ReadProcessMemory API, uses regex to find Track 1/Track 2 patterns. Persists via scheduled tasks and registry Run keys. Exfiltrates via FTP or HTTP POST. Cost attackers $2,000-3,000 on underground forums.

mitigations.title: Implement Point-to-Point Encryption (P2PE) for Payment Terminals
mitigations.description: Deploy PCI-validated Point-to-Point Encryption (P2PE) solutions that encrypt payment card data from the moment of card interaction (swipe/dip/tap) through the entire payment processing chain. P2PE-validated devices encrypt Track 1/Track 2 data in hardware before it ever enters system memory, completely eliminating the attack surface for RAM scrapers. This is the most effective defense against memory scraping attacks as there is no cleartext window for malware to exploit.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
A.14.1.2 - Securing application services on public networks
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
validation_steps: Verify payment terminal is PCI P2PE-validated (check PCI SSC website)
Confirm terminal uses SRED (Secure Reading and Exchange of Data)
Test that Track 1/Track 2 data is encrypted before leaving terminal hardware
Validate DUKPT key injection and key management procedures
Verify application never receives plaintext PAN in any form
Test memory scraping tools - should find no card data in RAM
Conduct PCI DSS assessment with P2PE scope reduction
Verify processor is PCI P2PE-validated decryption provider

mitigations.title: Deploy EDR with POS-Specific Memory Scraper Detection
mitigations.description: Implement Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) solutions specifically configured to detect POS malware behaviors including process memory scanning, Track 1/Track 2 pattern matching, and data exfiltration from retail environments. EDR should monitor for suspicious API calls (ReadProcessMemory, CreateToolhelp32Snapshot), regex pattern matching activity, and unauthorized network connections from POS terminals.

code_example.code:
# CrowdStrike Falcon Custom IOA (Indicator of Attack) Rules for POS Malware Detection
# Deploy via Falcon Console > Configuration > Custom IOA Rules

---
apiVersion: crowdstrike.com/v1
kind: CustomIOA
metadata:
  name: pos-memory-scraper-detection
  severity: Critical
  category: Malware
  platform: Windows
spec:
  description: |
    Detects memory scraping behavior characteristic of POS malware
    including Track 1/Track 2 regex pattern matching and ReadProcessMemory calls
  
  # Rule 1: Detect Track 1/Track 2 regex pattern usage
  pattern_matching:
    - name: track-data-regex-detection
      enabled: true
      severity: High
      conditions:
        process:
          commandline_regex: |
            # Track 1 pattern: %B followed by 13-19 digits
            .*%B[0-9]{13,19}.*
            # Track 2 pattern: ; followed by 13-19 digits
            .*;[0-9]{13,19}=[0-9]{4}.*
          excluded_processes:
            - "pos_application.exe"  # Legitimate POS app
            - "payment_processor.exe"
        actions:
          - alert:
              title: "POS Memory Scraper Regex Pattern Detected"
              description: "Process using Track 1/Track 2 regex patterns"
          - quarantine_process: true
          - collect_memory_dump: true

  # Rule 2: Detect suspicious ReadProcessMemory API calls
  api_monitoring:
    - name: readprocessmemory-monitoring
      enabled: true
      severity: High
      api_calls:
        - name: "ReadProcessMemory"
          filters:
            - target_process: 
                - "pos_application.exe"
                - "paymentprocessor.exe"
                - "aloha.exe"
                - "micros.exe"
            - calling_process_not_in:
                - "C:\\Windows\\System32\\"
                - "C:\\Program Files\\POSVendor\\"
            - call_frequency: "> 100 per minute"  # Rapid memory scanning
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "Suspicious Memory Access to POS Application"
            description: "Non-authorized process reading POS application memory"
        - kill_process: true
        - network_contain: true

  # Rule 3: Detect process enumeration (common in POS malware)
  process_enumeration:
    - name: createtoolhelp32snapshot-abuse
      enabled: true
      severity: Medium
      conditions:
        api_calls:
          - "CreateToolhelp32Snapshot"
          - "Process32First"
          - "Process32Next"
        followed_by:
          - "OpenProcess" with PROCESS_VM_READ | PROCESS_QUERY_INFORMATION
        within: "5 seconds"
        exclude_known_good:
          - "taskmgr.exe"
          - "procexp.exe"
          - "EDR_agent.exe"
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "Process Enumeration Followed by Memory Access"
            severity: High

  # Rule 4: Detect data exfiltration from POS terminals
  network_monitoring:
    - name: pos-data-exfiltration
      enabled: true
      severity: Critical
      conditions:
        network_connection:
          - direction: outbound
          - protocol: ["HTTP", "HTTPS", "DNS", "FTP"]
          - destination_not_in:
            - "known_payment_processors.txt"  # Whitelist legitimate processors
          - data_size: "> 1KB"  # Typical card dump size
        source_process:
          - path_contains: ["pos", "payment", "retail"]
          - or_signed_by_not: ["Verifone", "Ingenico", "PAX", "NCR"]
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "Suspicious Network Activity from POS System"
            description: "POS process communicating with unknown external host"
        - block_connection: true
        - isolate_host: true
        - create_forensic_snapshot: true

  # Rule 5: Detect persistence mechanisms used by POS malware
  persistence_detection:
    - name: pos-malware-persistence
      enabled: true
      severity: High
      conditions:
        registry_modification:
          - key: "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows\\CurrentVersion\\Run"
          - value_data_contains: ["pos", "payment", "retail"]
          - creating_process_not_signed: true
        or:
          scheduled_task_creation:
            - task_name_regex: ".*update.*|.*cache.*|.*service.*"
            - action_path_not_in:
              - "C:\\Windows\\System32\\"
              - "C:\\Program Files\\"
        or:
          service_installation:
            - service_name_suspicious: true
            - binary_path_writeable_by_users: true
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "POS Malware Persistence Mechanism Detected"
        - remove_persistence: true
        - quarantine_file: true

  # Rule 6: Detect known POS malware families by behavior
  malware_families:
    - name: blackpos-behavior
      signatures:
        - file_operations:
          - create_file: "C:\\Windows\\Temp\\*.tmp"
          - write_pattern: "^%B[0-9]{13,19}"  # Track 1 data
        - network:
          - ftp_connection: true
          - or_http_post: true
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "BlackPOS/Kaptoxa Malware Behavior Detected"
            severity: Critical
    
    - name: backoff-behavior
      signatures:
        - process_injection:
          - technique: "CreateRemoteThread"
          - target: "explorer.exe"
        - memory_scanning:
          - pattern: "Track 1/Track 2 regex"
          - interval: "< 500ms"
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "Backoff POS Malware Behavior Detected"
            severity: Critical
    
    - name: alina-behavior
      signatures:
        - string_operations:
          - base64_encode: true
          - xor_encryption: true
        - contains_string: ["track1", "track2", "cvv"]
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "Alina POS Malware Behavior Detected"
            severity: Critical

  # Rule 7: Memory forensics - scan for card data in process memory
  memory_forensics:
    - name: live-memory-card-data-scan
      enabled: true
      severity: Critical
      schedule: "every 15 minutes"
      scan_targets:
        - "pos_application.exe"
        - "all non-system processes"
      scan_patterns:
        - regex: "^%B[0-9]{13,19}\\^[^^]+\\^[0-9]{4}"  # Track 1
        - regex: ";[0-9]{13,19}=[0-9]{4}"  # Track 2
        - regex: "[0-9]{13,19}"  # PAN (Primary Account Number)
      exclude_if:
        - process_is: "pos_application.exe"
        - and_data_age: "< 5 seconds"  # Allow brief window for legitimate processing
      actions:
        - alert:
            title: "CRITICAL: Card Data Found in Process Memory"
            description: "Cleartext payment card data persisting in memory"
        - kill_process: true
        - memory_dump: true
        - incident_response: "escalate to SOC"

# Deploy Falcon sensor with custom IOA rules
---
deployment:
  sensor_version: "7.10+"
  update_policy: "automatic"
  prevention_policy:
    name: "POS_Terminals_High_Security"
    settings:
      - quarantine_on_write: true
      - network_containment: "automatic"
      - memory_protection: "enabled"
      - script_based_execution_monitoring: "enabled"
  
  exclusions:
    # Exclude legitimate POS applications from some checks
    processes:
      - path: "C:\\POSVendor\\Aloha\\*.exe"
        reason: "Legitimate Aloha POS application"
        excluded_from: ["process_enumeration"]
      - path: "C:\\Micros\\*.exe"
        reason: "Legitimate Oracle Micros POS"
        excluded_from: ["process_enumeration"]
  
  # Real-time response capabilities
  rtr_enabled: true
  forensics_collection: "automatic"
  
  # Integration with SIEM
  siem_integration:
    type: "Splunk"
    endpoint: "https://splunk.retail.com:8088/services/collector"
    events:
      - "DetectionSummaryEvent"
      - "ProcessExecutionEvent"
      - "NetworkConnectionEvent"
      - "MemoryForensicEvent"

# Response automation
---
incident_response:
  auto_remediation:
    - condition: "severity >= High AND confidence >= 80"
      actions:
        - isolate_host: true
        - kill_malicious_process: true
        - collect_forensics:
          - memory_dump
          - disk_image
          - network_pcap
        - notify:
          - email: "security-team@retail.com"
          - slack: "#security-incidents"
          - pagerduty: "P1-incident"
  
  forensics_retention: "90 days"
  compliance_reporting:
    - pci_dss: true
    - format: "PDF"
    - frequency: "quarterly"

nist_csf: DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
DE.CM-7 - Monitoring for unauthorized personnel, connections, devices, and software is performed
RS.AN-1 - Notifications from detection systems are investigated
validation_steps: Deploy EDR agent to all POS terminals and servers
Configure custom IOA rules for POS malware behaviors
Whitelist legitimate payment processor communications
Test detection with POS malware samples (in isolated environment)
Verify memory forensics scan detects Track 1/Track 2 patterns
Enable automatic containment and remediation
Integrate EDR alerts with SOC/SIEM platform
Conduct monthly detection efficacy testing

mitigations.title: Implement Network Segmentation and POS Isolation
mitigations.description: Isolate POS systems on dedicated network segments (VLANs) with strict firewall rules allowing only payment processor communication. Implement application-level whitelisting, disable unnecessary services, and use jump boxes for administrative access. This prevents lateral movement from compromised corporate networks to POS systems and restricts malware exfiltration channels.

code_example.code:
! Cisco ASA Firewall Configuration for POS Network Segmentation
! PCI DSS Requirement 1.2.1 and 1.3 compliant

!==========================================================================
! VLAN Configuration
!==========================================================================
interface GigabitEthernet0/0
 description Corporate Network
 nameif corporate
 security-level 50
 ip address 10.0.10.1 255.255.255.0
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 description POS Network (Cardholder Data Environment)
 nameif pos-cde
 security-level 100
 ip address 10.0.20.1 255.255.255.0
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/2
 description Payment Processor Network
 nameif payment-processor
 security-level 75
 ip address 10.0.30.1 255.255.255.0
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/3
 description POS Management Network (Jump Box)
 nameif pos-mgmt
 security-level 80
 ip address 10.0.40.1 255.255.255.0
!

!==========================================================================
! Object Groups for POS Systems and Payment Processors
!==========================================================================
object-group network POS-TERMINALS
 description All POS terminal IP addresses
 network-object 10.0.20.10 255.255.255.255  ! POS-Terminal-01
 network-object 10.0.20.11 255.255.255.255  ! POS-Terminal-02
 network-object 10.0.20.12 255.255.255.255  ! POS-Terminal-03
 network-object 10.0.20.13 255.255.255.255  ! POS-Terminal-04
 network-object 10.0.20.14 255.255.255.255  ! POS-Terminal-05
 network-object 10.0.20.0 255.255.255.0     ! Entire POS subnet
!
object-group network PAYMENT-PROCESSORS
 description Authorized payment processor IPs (whitelist)
 network-object host 203.0.113.10  ! Visa/Mastercard Processor
 network-object host 203.0.113.11  ! Shift4 P2PE Gateway
 network-object host 203.0.113.12  ! First Data
 network-object host 203.0.113.13  ! Worldpay Gateway
!
object-group service PAYMENT-SERVICES
 description Allowed payment processing services
 service-object tcp eq 443   ! HTTPS for payment authorization
 service-object tcp eq 8443  ! Alternate HTTPS port
!
object-group network POS-MANAGEMENT
 description Jump box for POS administration
 network-object host 10.0.40.10  ! Jump box IP
!

!==========================================================================
! Access Control Lists (ACLs) - Default Deny, Explicit Allow
!==========================================================================

! ACL 1: POS to Payment Processors ONLY (PCI DSS 1.2.1)
access-list POS-OUT extended permit tcp object-group POS-TERMINALS object-group PAYMENT-PROCESSORS object-group PAYMENT-SERVICES
access-list POS-OUT extended permit udp object-group POS-TERMINALS host 8.8.8.8 eq domain ! DNS for payment processor lookup
access-list POS-OUT extended deny ip any any log  ! Deny all other traffic and log
!
! ACL 2: Payment Processors to POS (responses only)
access-list PROCESSOR-IN extended permit tcp object-group PAYMENT-PROCESSORS object-group POS-TERMINALS established
access-list PROCESSOR-IN extended deny ip any any log
!
! ACL 3: Corporate network CANNOT directly access POS (PCI DSS 1.3.2)
access-list CORP-TO-POS extended deny ip any object-group POS-TERMINALS log
!
! ACL 4: Management access via Jump Box only (PCI DSS 1.3.7)
access-list MGMT-TO-POS extended permit tcp object-group POS-MANAGEMENT object-group POS-TERMINALS eq 3389  ! RDP
access-list MGMT-TO-POS extended permit tcp object-group POS-MANAGEMENT object-group POS-TERMINALS eq 22    ! SSH
access-list MGMT-TO-POS extended permit icmp object-group POS-MANAGEMENT object-group POS-TERMINALS echo   ! Ping
access-list MGMT-TO-POS extended deny ip any any log
!
! Apply ACLs to interfaces
access-group POS-OUT in interface pos-cde
access-group PROCESSOR-IN in interface payment-processor
access-group CORP-TO-POS in interface corporate
access-group MGMT-TO-POS in interface pos-mgmt

!==========================================================================
! Threat Detection and Inspection
!==========================================================================

! Enable threat detection for anomaly detection
threat-detection statistics access-list
threat-detection statistics port
threat-detection statistics protocol
threat-detection statistics tcp-intercept rate-interval 30 burst-rate 400 average-rate 200

! Scan for memory scraper exfiltration patterns
threat-detection scanning-threat shun duration 3600

! Deep packet inspection for payment data (alert if cleartext PAN detected)
class-map type inspect http match-all CLEARTEXT-PAN-DETECTION
 match request body regex class PAN-PATTERNS
!
regex PAN-PATTERNS "[0-9]{13,19}"  ! PAN pattern (should NEVER be in HTTP traffic)
!
policy-map type inspect http HTTP-PAYMENT-INSPECTION
 parameters
  protocol-violation action drop-connection log
 class CLEARTEXT-PAN-DETECTION
  drop-connection log
  ! Alert security team if cleartext PAN detected (possible exfiltration)
  log-level alert
!
policy-map global_policy
 class inspection_default
  inspect http HTTP-PAYMENT-INSPECTION
!
service-policy global_policy global

!==========================================================================
! Logging and Monitoring (PCI DSS 10.6.1)
!==========================================================================

! Send logs to SIEM for analysis
logging enable
logging timestamp
logging trap informational
logging host corporate 10.0.10.50 ! SIEM server IP
logging host corporate 10.0.10.51 ! Secondary SIEM

! Log all denied connections
logging message 106023 level informational ! Deny IP
logging message 106100 level informational ! Access denied
logging message 313001 level alert          ! Denied ICMP

! Log ACL hits for payment processor connections
logging class-of-service auth-inbound

! Enable NetFlow for traffic analysis
flow-export destination corporate 10.0.10.60 2055
flow-export template timeout-rate 1
flow-export active refresh-interval 1

!==========================================================================
! Additional Security Controls
!==========================================================================

! Disable unnecessary services on firewall
no service pad
no ip bootp server
no ip source-route
no ip finger
no ip http server
no ip http secure-server

! Enable unicast reverse path forwarding (anti-spoofing)
ip verify unicast source reachable-via rx

! Rate limiting to prevent DoS
police rate 10000000 burst 1000000

! Connection limits per host (prevent port scanning)
set connection conn-max 100 embryonic-conn-max 50

! Enable TCP sequence number randomization
tcp-map TCP-SECURITY-MAP
 tcp-options range 6 7 clear ! Remove timestamp options
 exceed-mss allow
!
class-map TCP-CLASS
 match default-inspection-traffic
!
policy-map TCP-POLICY
 class TCP-CLASS
  set connection advanced-options TCP-SECURITY-MAP
!
service-policy TCP-POLICY interface pos-cde

!==========================================================================
! Compliance Reporting
!==========================================================================

! Generate PCI DSS compliance reports
event manager applet PCI-COMPLIANCE-REPORT
 event timer cron cron-entry "0 0 * * 0"  ! Weekly on Sunday midnight
 action 1.0 cli command "show access-list | include deny"
 action 2.0 cli command "show logging | include denied"
 action 3.0 cli command "show conn count"
 action 4.0 syslog msg "PCI DSS Compliance Report Generated"
!

!==========================================================================
! Backup Configuration (encrypted)
!==========================================================================
archive
 log config
  logging enable
  notify syslog contenttype plaintext
 path ftp://admin:password@10.0.40.20/configs/asa-config-$h-$t
 write-memory
 time-period 1440  ! Daily backup
!

! END OF CONFIGURATION

!==========================================================================
! Validation Commands
!==========================================================================
! show access-list POS-OUT
! show conn all | include 10.0.20
! show service-policy global
! show logging | include POS
! show threat-detection statistics
! show flow-export

iso27001_controls: A.13.2.1 - Information transfer policies and procedures
nist_csf: PR.AC-5 - Network integrity is protected (e.g., network segregation, network segmentation)
PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
DE.CM-1 - The network is monitored to detect potential cybersecurity events
validation_steps: Create dedicated VLAN for POS systems (separate from corporate network)
Configure firewall to allow only payment processor IPs (whitelist)
Block all POS-to-corporate network traffic
Implement jump box for POS administrative access
Test that POS terminals cannot access internet or corporate resources
Verify firewall logs all denied connection attempts
Enable deep packet inspection for payment data patterns
Conduct quarterly PCI DSS network segmentation testing

mitigations.title: Enable Application Whitelisting and Disable Unnecessary Services
mitigations.description: Implement application whitelisting (Windows AppLocker, Carbon Black) on POS terminals to prevent execution of unauthorized binaries including memory scraper malware. Disable unnecessary Windows services (Remote Desktop, Windows Script Host, PowerShell) to reduce attack surface. This prevents malware installation and execution even if initial compromise occurs.

code_example.code:
# PowerShell script to configure POS terminal security
# Implements application whitelisting and service hardening
# PCI DSS Requirement 2.2 compliant

<#
.SYNOPSIS
    Hardens POS terminals against memory scraper malware
.DESCRIPTION
    Configures AppLocker, disables unnecessary services, and implements
    security controls for POS systems per PCI DSS requirements
#>

[CmdletBinding()]
param(
    [switch]$EnableAppLocker,
    [switch]$DisableServices,
    [switch]$AuditOnly
)

#========================================================================
# Application Whitelisting with AppLocker
#========================================================================

function Enable-POSApplicationWhitelisting {
    Write-Host "[*] Configuring AppLocker for POS terminals..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Create AppLocker policy XML
    $appLockerPolicy = @'
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<AppLockerPolicy Version="1">
  <!-- Executable Rules: Only allow signed executables from trusted publishers -->
  <RuleCollection Type="Exe" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <!-- Allow Windows System Files -->
    <FilePathRule Id="a61c8b2c-a319-4cd0-9690-d2177cad7b51" Name="Windows System" Description="Allow Windows system executables" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Allow">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="%WINDIR%\*" />
        <FilePathCondition Path="%PROGRAMFILES%\Windows Defender\*" />
      </Conditions>
    </FilePathRule>
    
    <!-- Allow specific POS applications ONLY -->
    <FilePublisherRule Id="fd686d83-a829-4351-8ff4-27c7de5755d2" Name="NCR Aloha POS" Description="Allow NCR Aloha POS application" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Allow">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePublisherCondition PublisherName="O=NCR Corporation" ProductName="Aloha" BinaryName="*">
          <BinaryVersionRange LowSection="*" HighSection="*" />
        </FilePublisherCondition>
      </Conditions>
    </FilePublisherRule>
    
    <FilePublisherRule Id="9c773b8a-d5ec-4c7c-ae2c-1f6e4f7d9c4f" Name="Oracle Micros POS" Description="Allow Oracle Micros POS" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Allow">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePublisherCondition PublisherName="O=Oracle Corporation" ProductName="Micros" BinaryName="*">
          <BinaryVersionRange LowSection="*" HighSection="*" />
        </FilePublisherCondition>
      </Conditions>
    </FilePublisherRule>
    
    <FilePathRule Id="c3c2b5d8-f7e9-4a3d-9c1f-8e7d6c5b4a3d" Name="Payment Terminal Software" Description="Allow payment terminal applications" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Allow">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="%PROGRAMFILES%\Verifone\*" />
        <FilePathCondition Path="%PROGRAMFILES%\Ingenico\*" />
        <FilePathCondition Path="%PROGRAMFILES%\PAX\*" />
      </Conditions>
    </FilePathRule>
    
    <!-- DENY ALL OTHER EXECUTABLES (default deny) -->
    <FilePathRule Id="b7af4782-9cea-4b10-a67b-4b2d9e8c6f9e" Name="Block All Others" Description="Deny execution of all other executables" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Deny">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="*" />
      </Conditions>
    </FilePathRule>
  </RuleCollection>
  
  <!-- Script Rules: Block PowerShell, VBS, JS, etc. -->
  <RuleCollection Type="Script" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <!-- Block ALL scripts (prevents fileless malware) -->
    <FilePathRule Id="06dce67b-934c-454f-a263-2515c8796a5d" Name="Block All Scripts" Description="Block PowerShell, VBS, JS, BAT scripts" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Deny">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="*" />
      </Conditions>
    </FilePathRule>
  </RuleCollection>
  
  <!-- DLL Rules: Only allow signed DLLs -->
  <RuleCollection Type="Dll" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <FilePathRule Id="86f235ad-3f7b-4121-bc95-ea8bde3a5bc5" Name="Windows DLLs" Description="Allow Windows system DLLs" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Allow">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePathCondition Path="%WINDIR%\*" />
        <FilePathCondition Path="%PROGRAMFILES%\*" />
      </Conditions>
    </FilePathRule>
  </RuleCollection>
  
  <!-- MSI Installer Rules: Block installations -->
  <RuleCollection Type="Msi" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <FilePublisherRule Id="5b290633-d17e-4fad-b7a3-7e4ed6e8c4c7" Name="Block Unauthorized Installers" Description="Block MSI installation except by IT" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-5-32-545" Action="Deny">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePublisherCondition PublisherName="*" ProductName="*" BinaryName="*">
          <BinaryVersionRange LowSection="*" HighSection="*" />
        </FilePublisherCondition>
      </Conditions>
    </FilePublisherRule>
  </RuleCollection>
  
  <!-- Packaged App Rules: Block Windows Store apps -->
  <RuleCollection Type="Appx" EnforcementMode="Enabled">
    <FilePublisherRule Id="b7af4782-9cea-4b10-a67b-4b2d9e8c6f9f" Name="Block Windows Store Apps" Description="Block all Windows Store apps" UserOrGroupSid="S-1-1-0" Action="Deny">
      <Conditions>
        <FilePublisherCondition PublisherName="*" ProductName="*" BinaryName="*">
          <BinaryVersionRange LowSection="*" HighSection="*" />
        </FilePublisherCondition>
      </Conditions>
    </FilePublisherRule>
  </RuleCollection>
</AppLockerPolicy>
'@
    
    # Save policy to temp file
    $policyPath = "$env:TEMP\POSAppLockerPolicy.xml"
    $appLockerPolicy | Out-File -FilePath $policyPath -Encoding UTF8
    
    # Import AppLocker policy
    try {
        if ($AuditOnly) {
            Write-Host "[!] Running in AUDIT mode - no enforcement" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Set-AppLockerPolicy -XmlPolicy $policyPath -Merge -ErrorAction Stop
        } else {
            Write-Host "[!] Applying AppLocker policy (ENFORCEMENT mode)" -ForegroundColor Yellow
            Set-AppLockerPolicy -XmlPolicy $policyPath -ErrorAction Stop
        }
        Write-Host "[+] AppLocker policy applied successfully" -ForegroundColor Green
    }
    catch {
        Write-Host "[!] Failed to apply AppLocker policy: $_" -ForegroundColor Red
        return $false
    }
    
    # Start AppLocker services
    Set-Service -Name AppIDSvc -StartupType Automatic
    Start-Service -Name AppIDSvc
    
    Write-Host "[+] AppLocker enabled and enforcing" -ForegroundColor Green
    return $true
}

#========================================================================
# Disable Unnecessary Services (Attack Surface Reduction)
#========================================================================

function Disable-UnnecessaryServices {
    Write-Host "[*] Disabling unnecessary services on POS terminal..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    $servicesToDisable = @(
        # Remote access services (prevent lateral movement)
        @{Name="RemoteRegistry"; Reason="Prevents remote registry access"},
        @{Name="RemoteAccess"; Reason="Prevents RAS/VPN access"},
        @{Name="SessionEnv"; Reason="Disables Remote Desktop session management"},
        @{Name="TermService"; Reason="Disables Remote Desktop Protocol"},
        @{Name="UmRdpService"; Reason="Disables RDP remote connections"},
        
        # Scripting and automation (prevents fileless malware)
        @{Name="WinRM"; Reason="Disables Windows Remote Management (PowerShell remoting)"},
        @{Name="WSearch"; Reason="Disables Windows Search (resource intensive)"},
        
        # Network services (reduces attack surface)
        @{Name="SNMP"; Reason="Disables SNMP (often exploited)"},
        @{Name="SNMPTRAP"; Reason="Disables SNMP Trap service"},
        @{Name="Browser"; Reason="Disables Computer Browser service"},
        @{Name="LanmanServer"; Reason="Disables file/printer sharing"},
        
        # Web services
        @{Name="W3SVC"; Reason="Disables IIS web server"},
        @{Name="WAS"; Reason="Disables Windows Process Activation Service"},
        
        # Bluetooth and wireless (not needed on POS)
        @{Name="bthserv"; Reason="Disables Bluetooth Support Service"},
        @{Name="WwanSvc"; Reason="Disables WWAN AutoConfig"},
        
        # Unnecessary Windows features
        @{Name="XblAuthManager"; Reason="Disables Xbox Live Auth Manager"},
        @{Name="XblGameSave"; Reason="Disables Xbox Live Game Save"},
        @{Name="XboxNetApiSvc"; Reason="Disables Xbox networking"}
    )
    
    foreach ($service in $servicesToDisable) {
        try {
            $svc = Get-Service -Name $service.Name -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
            if ($svc) {
                Stop-Service -Name $service.Name -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
                Set-Service -Name $service.Name -StartupType Disabled -ErrorAction Stop
                Write-Host "[+] Disabled: $($service.Name) - $($service.Reason)" -ForegroundColor Green
            }
        }
        catch {
            Write-Host "[!] Warning: Could not disable $($service.Name): $_" -ForegroundColor Yellow
        }
    }
    
    Write-Host "[+] Unnecessary services disabled" -ForegroundColor Green
}

#========================================================================
# Disable Scripting Engines (Prevent Fileless Malware)
#========================================================================

function Disable-ScriptingEngines {
    Write-Host "[*] Disabling scripting engines..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Disable Windows Script Host (prevents .vbs, .js execution)
    Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows Script Host\Settings" -Name "Enabled" -Value 0 -Type DWord -Force
    Write-Host "[+] Windows Script Host disabled" -ForegroundColor Green
    
    # Disable PowerShell v2 (old, vulnerable)
    Disable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName "MicrosoftWindowsPowerShellV2Root" -NoRestart
    Write-Host "[+] PowerShell v2 disabled" -ForegroundColor Green
    
    # Configure PowerShell Constrained Language Mode
    [Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable("__PSLockdownPolicy", "4", "Machine")
    Write-Host "[+] PowerShell Constrained Language Mode enabled" -ForegroundColor Green
    
    # Disable macros in Office (if Office installed)
    $officePath = "HKCU:\Software\Microsoft\Office\16.0"
    if (Test-Path $officePath) {
        Set-ItemProperty -Path "$officePath\Word\Security" -Name "VBAWarnings" -Value 4 -Type DWord -Force
        Set-ItemProperty -Path "$officePath\Excel\Security" -Name "VBAWarnings" -Value 4 -Type DWord -Force
        Write-Host "[+] Office macros disabled" -ForegroundColor Green
    }
}

#========================================================================
# Enable Security Auditing (PCI DSS 10.2)
#========================================================================

function Enable-SecurityAuditing {
    Write-Host "[*] Enabling security auditing..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
    
    # Enable process creation auditing (detect malware execution)
    auditpol /set /subcategory:"Process Creation" /success:enable /failure:enable
    
    # Enable command line auditing (detect PowerShell/cmd abuse)
    Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\System\Audit" `
        -Name "ProcessCreationIncludeCmdLine_Enabled" -Value 1 -Type DWord -Force
    
    # Enable AppLocker auditing
    auditpol /set /subcategory:"Application Generated" /success:enable /failure:enable
    
    # Enable credential access auditing
    auditpol /set /subcategory:"Credential Validation" /success:enable /failure:enable
    
    Write-Host "[+] Security auditing enabled" -ForegroundColor Green
}

#========================================================================
# Main Execution
#========================================================================

Write-Host "\n=============================================================" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  POS Terminal Security Hardening Script" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "  PCI DSS Requirement 2.2 Compliant" -ForegroundColor Cyan
Write-Host "=============================================================\n" -ForegroundColor Cyan

if ($EnableAppLocker) {
    Enable-POSApplicationWhitelisting
}

if ($DisableServices) {
    Disable-UnnecessaryServices
    Disable-ScriptingEngines
}

Enable-SecurityAuditing

# Verify configuration
Write-Host "\n[*] Verifying configuration..." -ForegroundColor Cyan
$appLockerStatus = Get-AppLockerPolicy -Effective -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if ($appLockerStatus) {
    Write-Host "[+] AppLocker is active" -ForegroundColor Green
} else {
    Write-Host "[!] WARNING: AppLocker is NOT active!" -ForegroundColor Red
}

Write-Host "\n[+] POS terminal hardening complete!" -ForegroundColor Green
Write-Host "[!] REBOOT REQUIRED for all changes to take effect" -ForegroundColor Yellow

# Example execution:
# .\Harden-POSTerminal.ps1 -EnableAppLocker -DisableServices
# .\Harden-POSTerminal.ps1 -EnableAppLocker -AuditOnly  # Test mode

nist_csf: PR.IP-3 - Configuration change control processes are in place
PR.PT-3 - The principle of least functionality is incorporated
validation_steps: Deploy AppLocker policy to all POS terminals via Group Policy
Configure policy in audit mode first, review logs for legitimate apps
Whitelist only required POS applications (NCR Aloha, Oracle Micros, etc.)
Enable enforcement mode after validation period
Test malware samples - should be blocked from execution
Disable all unnecessary Windows services
Verify PowerShell and WSH are disabled/restricted
Monitor AppLocker event logs (Event IDs 8003, 8004)

indicators: Process creating regex patterns for Track 1 (%B[0-9]{13,19}) or Track 2 (;[0-9]{13,19}=) data
Repeated ReadProcessMemory API calls targeting payment application processes
Process enumeration via CreateToolhelp32Snapshot followed by OpenProcess with PROCESS_VM_READ
Outbound network connections from POS terminals to non-whitelisted IPs
Creation of suspicious files in %TEMP% or %APPDATA% directories on POS systems
FTP or HTTP POST traffic from POS terminals
DNS queries to suspicious domains from POS systems
Registry modifications for persistence (Run keys, scheduled tasks)
Unsigned or unexpected executables running on POS terminals
Base64-encoded data in network traffic from POS systems
Memory consumption spikes from payment application processes
Cleartext payment card data (Track 1/Track 2/PAN) found in process memory dumps

log_queries.query:
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Security host=POS-*
(EventCode=4688 OR EventCode=4689)
| eval CommandLine=lower(CommandLine)
| where match(CommandLine, "%b[0-9]{13,19}") OR match(CommandLine, ";[0-9]{13,19}=")
| stats count by host, User, NewProcessName, CommandLine, _time
| where count > 1
| table _time, host, User, NewProcessName, CommandLine
| sort - _time

index=firewall sourcetype=cisco:asa src_ip=10.0.20.*
| where NOT (dest_ip IN ("203.0.113.10", "203.0.113.11", "203.0.113.12")) 
| stats count as ConnectionCount, values(dest_ip) as Destinations, sum(bytes_out) as TotalBytes
  by src_ip, dest_port
| where ConnectionCount > 5 OR TotalBytes > 10000
| eval Severity=case(
    TotalBytes > 100000, "Critical",
    TotalBytes > 50000, "High",
    1=1, "Medium"
  )
| table src_ip, dest_port, Destinations, ConnectionCount, TotalBytes, Severity
| sort - TotalBytes

// Detect POS memory scraper behaviors
SecurityEvent
| where Computer startswith "POS-"
| where EventID == 4688  // Process creation
| extend ProcessName = tostring(split(NewProcessName, "\\")[-1])
| where ProcessName !in ("aloha.exe", "micros.exe", "verifone.exe", "pos_app.exe")
| extend HasTrackPattern = CommandLine matches regex @"(?i)(%B[0-9]{13,19}|;[0-9]{13,19}=)"
| where HasTrackPattern == true
| project
    TimeGenerated,
    Computer,
    Account,
    NewProcessName,
    CommandLine,
    ParentProcessName
| order by TimeGenerated desc

sequence by host.name with maxspan=5m
  [process where process.name != "pos_application.exe" and
   process.command_line regex~ ".*(CreateToolhelp32Snapshot|Process32First).*"]
  [process where process.name != "pos_application.exe" and
   process.command_line regex~ ".*(ReadProcessMemory|OpenProcess).*"]
  [network where destination.ip != "203.0.113.*" and bytes_sent > 1000]

fields @timestamp, @message, host, process, network.destination
| filter host like /POS-/
| filter @message like /ReadProcessMemory/ or @message like /Track/
| stats count() as SuspiciousActivity by host, process
| filter SuspiciousActivity > 10
| sort SuspiciousActivity desc

tools.name: Volatility Framework
tools.usage: Analyze memory dumps for RAM scraper artifacts, Track 1/Track 2 patterns, and malware signatures

tools.name: Wireshark
tools.usage: Capture and analyze POS network traffic for cleartext payment data exfiltration

tools.name: ProcDump
tools.usage: Create memory dumps of POS processes for forensic analysis

tools.name: FireEye HX
tools.usage: Real-time detection of POS malware behaviors and memory scraping

compliance: PCI DSS 4.0 (Requirement 3.5.1 - PAN not stored in volatile memory)
